and the requirements gathering conversation, draft a values document for the Flight Recorder project. ast.raw-value: DONE Refine SemanticLogger/SecretClassifier Foundation Design Decision
title: DONE Refine SemanticLogger/SecretClassifier Foundation Design Decision

ast: Coordinate with Tools team and validate technical foundation assumptions for SemanticLogger/SecretClassifier integration before Friday Platform meeting.
requires immediate coordination and technical validation to avoid conflicts with Tools team work. Priority actions: (1) Explore

, and other key files to assess maturity of this tool. Is it actively in use? Is it architecturally sound? Is it a "ready to go" component for the Flight Recorder implementation, or do we have some enhancements to do?  (2) Examine the actual codebase usage to determine if these patterns are already being used in production services or still in development. (3) Investigate performance characteristics of SecretClassifier.classify() operations and StateProvider storage overhead for separated public/secret log storage. (4) Validate the integration complexity between SemanticLogger, SecretClassifier, and StateProvider - are there known integration patterns or will FlightRecorder be the first comprehensive user? (5) Define the data flow architecture precisely - how do the structured logs move from classification through StateProvider to export? (6) Address the privacy compliance questions around encryption key management for secret log data. Update the design decision with concrete technical findings and create a stakeholder coordination plan that ensures Tools team alignment before presenting this architecture to the Friday Platform meeting.

This baseline understanding is critical for designing the Flight Recorder enhancement strategy.

ast: Define concrete milestones that balance quick wins with long-term vision.
, we need to go ahead and set some milestones now. This is what I'm thinking for v0:
We release an Export Logs button that
Fetches runtime available diagnostic information: the client, version, OS, and feature flag status.
Saves that information to a txt file for the user

No implementation of FlightRecorder, SemanticLogger, SecretClassifier, StateProvider, nothin. Just easily available process data and a UI. We can still program in a future-thinking way with this context in mind.
When the user clicks Export Logs a simple confirmation dialog appears
After that, I'm not sure where to take it. Brainstorm with me.
Here's our milestone plan so far. It's short, but we still have some research to do.
We still have these unanswered questions:
What's the IO burden on Desktop for writing logs to data.json constantly?
How long do we want to keep logs? How large do we want the maximum size of our logs to be?

title: DONE Establish Concrete Performance Guarantees for FlightRecorderLayer

ast: Create measurable performance assertions and benchmarks proving FlightRecorderLayer has negligible overhead compared to the problematic performance
, a reviewer raised a critical concern about performance on our

I think it might be a good idea to add a small mention to logging performance here somewhere. The current design already does great by ensuring that the log layer's only job is putting the events into a circular buffer (which should be very fast), and the future improvements seem to indicate that any extra processing on the logs would be made asynchronously, but I think it would be a good idea to add a guarantee somewhere that the code path of our
should be kept as lean and performant as possible.
used in WASM at the moment is creating some performance problems during decryption: [Slack link] And KM has been lookin into putting it behind a feature flag: [PR #738]
I'd like to make sure that we don't accidentally design us into a performance sink, specially in the future when we start implementing log encryption/persistence
Just adding a design principle without concrete proof is useless - I need you to establish measurable performance guarantees. Create criterion benchmarks following the pattern in
that measure the three critical paths: event conversion (
flow. Target <1μs per operation on typical hardware.

This concrete data will prove we've learned from the performance
issues. I'll update Confluence with your findings.

). This is our enrichment pattern: fields passed to tracing macros automatically become part of the event.
